misconceptionSummary: Students treat division as commutative, writing a ÷ b = b ÷ a.

steps.narration: Multiplication is commutative — swapping the order gives the same product. Four times seven and seven times four are both twenty-eight.

lines.text: a ÷ b ≠ b ÷ a  in general

steps.narration: Division is not commutative. Twenty divided by four is five, but four divided by twenty is one fifth. Swapping the numbers gives a completely different result.

lines.text: Multiplication and division undo each other
One multiplication gives two division facts

steps.narration: Multiplication and division are inverses. Every multiplication fact gives two division facts for free. Six times eight is forty-eight, so forty-eight divided by eight is six, and forty-eight divided by six is eight.

visuals.expression: a × b = b × a,  but  a ÷ b ≠ b ÷ a
visuals.label: Multiplication: commutative. Division: order matters.

steps.narration: Multiplication is commutative; division is not. And the two are inverses — each undoes the other.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 4 ÷ 20 = 20 ÷ 4 = 5. Division is not commutative — 4 ÷ 20 = 0.2, not 5. Only multiplication allows free swapping of the two numbers.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: You can swap numbers in multiplication but never in division. Four divided by twenty and twenty divided by four give completely different answers.
